# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark results, implementation choices for a SWE-bench Verified agent, negative findings about techniques that did not help, and explicit caveats about SWE-bench as a proxy for real-world software engineering.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: metric
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Augment reports a 65.4% success rate on SWE-bench Verified.

## Source Excerpt

“Today, we are proud to share that we have achieved a 65.4% success rate on SWE-bench verified.”

## Note

This is a self-reported benchmark result from the source.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The reported 65.4% SWE-bench Verified submission used Claude Sonnet 3.7 as the core driver and OpenAI o1 as the ensembler.

## Source Excerpt

“To achieve a 65.4% success rate on our first-ever SWE-bench submission we combined Claude Sonnet 3.7 as our core driver, along with OpenAI’s o1 as our ensembler.”

## Note

This evidence states the model combination used for the reported submission.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Augment says its main implementation differences from Anthropic’s published SWE-bench setup were inferring the unpublished planning tool and using OpenAI o1 for ensembling.

## Source Excerpt

“The main deltas in our implementation include figuring out what their unpublished ‘planning’ tool was and using OpenAI’s o1 model as our ensembler.”

## Note

This is a direct statement of the source’s claimed implementation deltas.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: result
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Augment reports that Claude Sonnet 3.7 thinking mode did not help on SWE-bench.

## Source Excerpt

“We were surprised to see some techniques that didn’t help such as Sonnet 3.7’s thinking mode...”

## Note

This is a negative finding about a tested technique and creates tension with any assumption that more explicit thinking automatically improves benchmark performance.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: result
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
A separate “fix regressions” agent produced no net score improvement because it fixed some regressions but also introduced bugs into otherwise correct candidate solutions.

## Source Excerpt

“We found this direction of research ultimately wasn’t very fruitful either. While the ‘fix regressions’ agent was able to find and fix some regressions, it also introduced bugs into otherwise correct candidates solutions, resulting in no net improvement to the final score.”

## Note

This evidence preserves both the partial benefit and the offsetting failure mode.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: metric
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Augment estimates that ensembling can add 3% to 8% on SWE-bench Verified.

## Source Excerpt

“In addition to this, there is a gain of 3-8% to be had from ensembling techniques.”

## Note

This is an internal estimate reported by the source, not a separately documented experiment table.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: claim-support
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Augment says agent outcomes were unstable enough that any two rollouts sampled over 50 examples could differ in outcome.

## Source Excerpt

“We found that when sampling any two of our rollouts over 50 examples would have different outcomes between the two rollouts.”

## Note

The sentence is grammatically rough in the source, but it clearly asserts rollout instability as the reason ensembling can help.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: result
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
For SWE-bench tasks, Augment found “grep” and “find” sufficient, and embedding-based retrieval was not the bottleneck.

## Source Excerpt

“We explored adding various embedding-based retrieval tools but found that for SWE-bench tasks this was not the bottleneck – ‘grep’ and ‘find’ were sufficient.”

## Note

This is scoped to SWE-bench tasks, not all production coding settings.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: result
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Augment says the same “grep” and “find” navigation approach has limitations in real-world use because of ambiguous user inputs and large codebases.

## Source Excerpt

“However, while this codebase navigation technique works well for SWE-bench, we found that for real world use-cases, this approach to codebase awareness currently has limitations, due to its ability to handle ambiguous user inputs and large codebases.”

## Note

This evidence creates a benchmark-to-production tension: the method works on SWE-bench but is reported as limited in broader deployment settings.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: metric
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The source cites OpenAI’s finding that only 8.4% of SWE-bench Verified problems take an experienced software engineer more than an hour to solve.

## Source Excerpt

“Finally, OpenAI found that only 8.4% of problems in SWE-bench Verified take more than an hour to solve by an experienced software engineer.”

## Note

This is presented as a benchmark limitation and should remain attached to any downstream use of SWE-bench difficulty claims.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: incident
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The source states that SWE-bench only includes Python projects.

## Source Excerpt

“It also only includes Python projects, missing the diversity of languages in actual development environments.”

## Note

This is a benchmark-scope caveat that limits cross-language generalization.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: claim-support
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The source argues that Python is easier for agents than Java or C++ because failed-test error messages tend to be more descriptive.

## Source Excerpt

“Consider how error messages from failed tests tend to be significantly more descriptive in Python than in languages like Java and C++, which makes Python an easier language for agents to work with.”

## Note

This is an argued limitation stated in the source, not a quantified cross-language experiment.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-015
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: result
citation: Augment Code blog, Mar. 31, 2025
source_url: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/1-open-source-agent-on-swe-bench-verified-by-combining-claude-3-7-and-o1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Augment reports that some changes that improved its production agents in qualitative customer feedback did not improve SWE-bench scores.

## Source Excerpt

“We found countless other examples like this where changes that improved the quality of our production coding agents (as measured by qualitative customer feedback) did not move the needle on SWE-bench.”

## Note

This preserves a direct tension between benchmark gains and reported production-quality gains.

Augment says the same “grep” and “find” navigation approach has limitations in real-world use because of ambiguous user inputs and large codebases.

## Source Excerpt

“However, while this codebase navigation technique works well for SWE-bench, we found that for real world use-cases, this approach to codebase awareness currently has limitations, due to its ability to handle ambiguous user inputs and large codebases.”

## Note

This evidence creates a benchmark-to-production tension: the method works on SWE-bench but is reported as limited in broader deployment settings.
```

This source contains benchmark results, implementation choices for a SWE-bench Verified agent, negative findings about techniques that did not help, and explicit caveats about SWE-bench as a proxy for real-world software engineering.

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown

mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
A separate “fix regressions” agent produced no net score improvement because it fixed some regressions but also introduced bugs into otherwise correct candidate solutions.

## Source Excerpt

“We found this direction of research ultimately wasn’t very fruitful either. While the ‘fix regressions’ agent was able to find and fix some regressions, it also introduced bugs into otherwise correct candidates solutions, resulting in no net improvement to the final score.”

## Note

This evidence preserves both the partial benefit and the offsetting failure mode.
```
